findings.reachability: Any caller of simpleGet() or simpleGet.concat() that passes a user-controlled URL string or opts.url property
findings.justification: opts.url is passed directly into url.parse() and the resulting hostname/port/path are forwarded to http.request()/https.request() without any validation against private IP ranges, loopback addresses, or cloud metadata endpoints (e.g. 169.254.169.254), enabling full SSRF.

detection_pattern.description: Detects direct use of http.request/https.request or url.parse without any private-IP or allowlist guard, which enables SSRF when the URL is user-controlled.

findings.reachability: Any caller making a request to a server that can issue 3xx redirects; the attacker controls the redirect Location header
findings.justification: On 3xx responses, res.headers.location is assigned to opts.url and the recursive simpleGet() call is made without re-validating the new URL against private IP ranges, so a redirect from a public server to http://127.0.0.1/ or http://169.254.169.254/ succeeds.

detection_pattern.description: Detects redirect-following code that does not re-validate the Location header URL, allowing an attacker-controlled redirect target to reach internal services.

findings.reachability: Callers passing a user-controlled URL string; affected in older Node.js versions where net/http do not strip CRLF
findings.justification: The path component extracted by url.parse(opts.url) is used verbatim in the HTTP request; CRLF characters in the path (%0d%0a) can inject extra HTTP headers in Node.js versions that do not sanitize the path, enabling request smuggling or header injection.

detection_pattern.description: Detects use of raw path values from url.parse without CRLF stripping, which can allow header injection in older Node.js runtimes.

findings.reachability: Any caller who passes opts.headers sourced from untrusted input (e.g. parsed JSON or user-supplied object)
findings.justification: opts.headers keys are iterated with Object.keys() and copied into a plain headers object without filtering __proto__ or constructor; an input like { "__proto__": { "admin": true } } pollutes Object.prototype via the bracket-notation assignment headers[k.toLowerCase()] = opts.headers[k].

detection_pattern.section_name: Prototype pollution via header key merge
detection_pattern.description: Detects loops that copy arbitrary keys from a user-supplied object into a plain object using bracket notation, which can pollute Object.prototype when __proto__ or constructor keys are present.

findings.reachability: Callers of simpleGet.concat() with opts.json=true making requests to attacker-controlled or compromised servers
findings.justification: JSON.parse() is called on the raw HTTP response body without any schema validation; a malicious server can return a crafted JSON payload that, when later passed to recursive merge utilities by the caller, enables prototype pollution or type confusion downstream.

detection_pattern.description: Detects JSON.parse calls on raw HTTP response data without schema validation or a reviver that blocks prototype-sensitive keys, flagging a deserialization trust boundary issue.

findings.reachability: Callers passing opts.headers with values sourced from user input
findings.justification: Header values from opts.headers are copied verbatim into the outgoing request without CRLF sanitization; in runtimes that do not enforce HTTP/1.1 header field rules, an attacker-controlled header value containing \r\n can inject arbitrary headers or split the HTTP request.

detection_pattern.description: Detects assignment of user-controlled values to HTTP header fields without stripping CRLF sequences, which enables header injection or HTTP request splitting.
